speaker: interviewee
text: --talk a little bit about the children, and then-- then they would--

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I have to take you back to-- to the DP camp. I can't do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are interviewing Mira Shelub. And we are at tape number five. Mira, when we left of the prior tape, you are past liberation now. And you have made your way to Bad Gastein in Austria. And this is a displaced persons camp. And describe arriving to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Bad Gastein. Bad Gastein was a German resort, where Hitler used to come and rest. We were placed in beautiful hotels. And everybody felt really bad about. Because they felt we went through so much, in the forest, in the concentration camps. And here they were enjoying themselves in these beautiful hotels. So-- but we started to acclimatize ourselves. We started to-- to-- to work a little bit. I-- I worked at an office for a German-- for a, sorry, for an American woman as an interpreter. And I was helping the survivors leave for America or Israel. I helped him to have all these papers in order, to-- also to make contact with their relatives in America. They would get letters from America in English. And I would read it to them, translate it-- the letters into Yiddish, into Polish. My husband, he organized the partisan movement in Bad Gastein. And we were demonstrating on the streets of Bad Gastein, demanding independence for Israel, Israeli independence. We lived in nice hotels. We would get the food from UNRRA. UNRRA supplied us with the food. We would enjoy the-- the beautiful view in-- in Bad Gastein. Let's face it, it's a beautiful resort. It was something very special. We would walk around. We would look around. But mainly, we would dream about leaving the DP camps and coming somewhere where we can settle down and lead a normal-- normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the decision made to go from Prague, which is where you were at that time, to Bad Gastein?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- we came to Prague. We were situated, again, in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] different quarters. We-- by-- as far as I remember, by cars we left Prague for Bad Gastein. Were driven from Prague to Bad Gastein.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- by the-- by UNRRA. UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the decision wasn't yours to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- we were anxious to reach the Americans zone. And then-- and then UNRRA took care of the rest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were placed-- from Prague, we were placed in Bad Gastein in these hotels. And then we started to work on our papers, in order to have everything in order, in order to be able to leave Bad Gastein for United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: We personally, we were dreaming to go to Israel. And we were counting the borders, how many borders were left to cross. We were very active partisans, felt good about our-- our past. In other words, the people who were put in the-- in the concentration camps, they, I mean, they had no choice. That's what's happened. But with us, we knew that we were fighting against the-- the Germans. We knew that we fought for our loved ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we felt that we should organize ourselves. And that's when we-- my husband organized the group in Bad Gastein, the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say that we are-- that we are better than the ones who were put into concentration camps. But it happened that from our areas, they did not put us in concentration camps. But we-- or they finished us up right there on the spot, or we escaped to the forest. But the ones who lived, you know, in, you know, Warsaw, deeper into Poland, they were forced to go to-- to-- to-- to go to concentration camps. So that was the difference between us and the survivors of the concentration camps. I brought it up, because in Bad Gastein, we had people from all over-- from all over Poland, people from-- from different European countries. And they were all in-- settled in displaced persons camp. In matter of fact, that-- we had some people from Chelm. Chelm is a city in Poland. According to the Yiddish literature, all the-- the people who lived in Chelm were considered being stupid. And-- so-- SO when we looked at the people and we knew that they are from Chelm, we looked down to them. We felt something is wrong about them. But nothing was wrong about them. They were as smart as we were. But you felt that something is wrong, because they come from Chelm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were people from all over, from all over. And then, little by little, they started to leave for Israel, for America. And we-- once we made contact with our relatives, we were also getting ready to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were also the dreaming about going back to school with my husband. And at that point, my friends-- some of my friends, they went to-- they left for Germany, where they were attending a German university. I with my husband, we also wanted to go. But we were afraid we will lose the quota for America. So we did not go. But our friends, they did go. They finished school over there in Germany. And-- and-- and then they left for America later. It was OK. They included the quota.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we finally left for the United States. We came to United States. How did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did we-- how? We-- we went on a ship. And I was so sick. Because I got-- you know, I had this seasick-- seasickness. And it was just terrible. I thought I'll never come to America. The captain-- the captain gave us some lemons and to-- I said, you shouldn't give to me. It's just a waste. Because I'll never survive anyway. I'll never be able to come to America. But somehow survived. And we came to America. Our relatives were waiting for us. We first arrived-- we came to New York. Our relatives were in New Rochelle, New Rochelle, New York. We stayed there for I would say about two months. And then we left for San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you get that far, I want to go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to describe when you first saw the-- the shores of the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: The shores, the shores were not that-- the shores, it's-- I wasn't-- I was impressed. I was impressed. But mainly I was impressed when I came to San Francisco, to our relatives. And I felt that I am already in heaven. I have everything. I-- I-- I saw the way they-- they-- they lived, the way they had everything arranged. I felt, my gosh, I am just in heaven. It's beautiful. Only my brother and sister are still in Germany. So I started to send them packages. And I would send them food-- prunes and-- and peaches in the cans. And they told me later that they-- when they received the peaches and the-- and the-- and the prunes, they would sit down, and just didn't say a word and just ate up the whole thing. They were so enthused with the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- I-- I liked it here very much. It was hard to find a place at the beginning. We lived with my-- with my husband's uncle and aunt. It was hard for my husband to find work.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was dreaming about going back to school, but naturally he couldn't, because we had a one-year-old son. I was dreaming about going to school. And I had to take care of the baby. But my husband, he was involved with Heald College, here in San Francisco, where he would receive from them some lectures, and he would do some homework at home. But it was very hard on him, because he had to go to school, and he had to work. So finally he temporarily gave up. And so did I, so did I. He started-- he-- he worked for his uncle. It was very hard. He was not exactly satisfied. That was not his dream. He-- he wanted to become a professional. He wanted to go back to school. So-- but then he felt that he would like to get into his own business. And we finally got in-- into-- into our own business. We had a restaurant, a fast food store at the Embarcadero Center. And they called it [? Norman's ?] Institution. Because everyone would come into the store and say he's hungry, my husband would assure him that when he leaved the store, he will not be hungry. So it was very successful business. And everything worked out OK. We came here-- I would like now to tell you a little bit about my children, and when we came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to wait before you tell me about your children. Because we haven't finished with you yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like first for you to go back to New York. How long did you stay in New Rochelle?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, in New Rochelle we stayed two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, we were just visiting with our relatives. Visiting with our relatives, looking at pictures. They would love to listen to our stories, love to listen to the way we survived the war, loved to listen about the families who did not survive, who perished during the Second World War. They would take-- we would go visit one relative, another relative. And just spent time with them and talking about the past.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel in relation to your relatives? Did you feel uncomfortable that you were different, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- we-- I-- I felt-- I felt good. Those were actually my husband's relatives. And they treated us very nicely. And it was good to be with them. But naturally, we longed to come to a place where we can finally settle down, where we can start to-- to-- to live our own lives, to-- to work, and to-- and to go-- go on living!

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you decide to come to-- to San Francisco?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband had a big family in San Francisco. So they wanted us just to come to San Francisco. We never intended to stay in New Rochelle. We felt that we'll go to San Francisco and-- and stay here for a short time and then go to Israel. But we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came here.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to San Francisco? When we came to San Francisco, I still had this unconscious desire of going back to school. But I-- I didn't know how that could materialize. Because I had my baby, and I had to take care of my little son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son was born where?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Salzburg, where we were in Salzburg. And when we came here. He was just a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [? ltzrachel, ?] [? Itzrach. ?] When we came here to America, I would walk with him in the park, and people would ask me, what's his name? And I couldn't say [? Itzrach ?] because, to them, it didn't mean anything, [? Itzrach. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So one week I called him Michael. One week I called him Eddie. Finally, we came once home, and we talked to my husband, and we said we have to decide. How are we going to call him? So we made our minds. And we said, we are going to call him Irwin. And that-- and even for me, it's hard to prominence Irwin. So I remember when he started going to school, the teacher would always send a note and say, how do you-- how do you pronounce it? How do you spell it? 'Irwin' or 'Erwin'? Because I wouldn't pronounce it properly. I would say, 'Erwin,' and it's 'Irwin.' So we had lots of fun. But-- and he was a beautiful baby, with blond curly hair. And everybody would stop him on the street and say is that a boy or a girl? And finally I cut his hair, for he was a boy. So you couldn't go to school you thought?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could-- I could not go to school. And it was hurting a lot. And so did my husband, he couldn't either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started-- we-- he was working for-- OK, he was working for-- for his uncle in the furniture store. I-- [SIGHS] I was involved in Venetian glass. I was selling Venetian glass to-- wholesale to stores, to furniture stores, to flower shops. Who-- whoever carried it, I would sell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one who supplied me with the Venetian glass was Max Schiff-- Max [? Sucher. ?] He lives in Los Angeles now. So he would supply me with the items, and I would to sell it. I was like a middleman. And I developed a nice little business. I would drive to different places and sell these items. Finally, my husband, he wanted badly to get into his own business. And we decided that we'll do it. And that's when I gave up my business, and he got out of this furniture store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we opened up our own restaurant. And it was a big success. We did real well. Can I tell you a little bit about my children? Yes, now I know you have more children.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So when we came to America, and we built up a nice business. And we raised a beautiful family. I have two sons and a daughter. My oldest son, he was born in Austria, in Salzburg. When we came here, he was a year old. He went to-- to elementary school, than to junior high, high school. And finally, he graduated from Berkeley University. Then he applied to medical school, to Baylor Medical in Houston. He graduated, he became a very successful, busy doctor. He's a lung specialist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other son?

speaker: interviewee
text: That will come. And he-- he has his wife and two sons. And his-- my-- his older son is at Santa Cruz University. And I am very proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandchildren? Aaron and Nathan. Nathan is named after my husband. Then I have my daughter. My daughter, she lives in Michigan, Detroit, Michigan. She graduated from UC Davis with a master's in-- in social work. And she has a good position in the health industry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elaine, Elaine, Elaine. And she has a friend, and she's happy there. And I'm very proud of her. Now my youngest son, Mark. Mark was going to different schools, my youngest one. And he-- he was a very good student. He graduated from elementary school, junior high, high school with straight As. Then he went to Yale University, where he graduated with magna cum laude. And he applied to medical school. And he graduated from medical school, UCLA. His specialty is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] rehabilitation. And-- and he's healing patients with back pains.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, it's-- he now got two tables from Canada. And he's going-- OK. He is going to treat people with these tables. So I'm very proud of him. I'm very proud of my children. They are doing fine. And everything is OK. But-- can I continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we came to-- to America, and-- and we built up a nice business, raised as you-- you heard a beautiful family. And then in 1977, we suffered a great tragedy. I lost my life companion of 37 years. He was 57 years old. He had open heart surgery, and he never made it. At that point, I felt that my life is over. I could not see how I will possibly be able to go on without-- without him. I could not see the light at the end of the tunnel. But with the help of my children, I returned to school. I worked very hard. In 1988, I got my bachelor in history, and in 1994, my master's in counseling. And because I speak Russian, I counsel Russian immigrants. I feel that I can understand them. I feel that we have a very similar playground. I feel I can sit and listen to them and talk to them. When I come back home from my clients, I count my blessings. I feel that I am OK. I am OK. I just help. I have to help my clients. And I do, and I do, and I do help them. I also teach Yiddish at three different locations in San Francisco. Yiddish that I acquired at my grammar school, in the shtetl, and Yiddish that I studied in gymnasium in Vilna. And while I'm-- while I'm here in San Francisco, I also studied Yiddish literature at Columbia University in New York. And I lived on the campus, and it was quite an experience. And I also-- I also studied Yiddish literature at Oxford, in England, where I was dwelling on Yiddish literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '87, 1987. That-- it was a wonderful experience. I met people from all over the world. I met people from-- students from all over the world. We were visiting. We were sitting and talking to teach other. It was a wonderful experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I am leading three groups in San Francisco, where I'm teaching Yiddish, conversational Yiddish. And that is at the Holocaust Center, one, one at the Jewish Community Center Brotherhood Way, and one at Congregation Ner Tamid. And it's satisfying. It's revealing. It feels good. So that how I spend my time. And now I would like to tell you that in the forest, we not only fought a physical battle, but we also fought a spiritual battle. As we sat around the fire, sitting together, singing songs together, supporting each other, helping each other, dreaming about better days, a better tomorrow, and a better future. And I would like, if I can do it, to read to you a short paragraph that helped me so much. I would like to read that to you. And I would also like to read to you a verse from the partisan hymn that we sang in the forest. Can I do it? Very short. We'll take a break, and we'll pick up with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mira, we're picking up with your-- where we last left off, with your telling me about your children. And now I'd like to give you the opportunity, if you left out any part of your story that you'd like to tell us, if there's anything that you forgot while we were talking. Now would be your opportunity to tell us. And also, if you have any message that you want to leave the world with, that you want to tell anybody about your experience overall, how you feel, or what message you want to get across to the people, this would be the time to tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I forgot to tell you that I am working for the Jewish Family and Children Service. It's a nice organization. It's really helping a lot to people who come here from Russia, Russian immigrants. And I am part of it. I'm proud to be part of it. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] What I would like to tell the world is that we fought during the war as partisans, against the enemy. Taking revenge for our loved ones, who perished in such a horrible, unbelievable manner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I believe that in order to have peace, we have to learn to live in peace with people. We cannot forget what's happened. But we have to learn-- never forget, never. But we have to learn to forgive, so peace might come. I remember that when I was visiting with my son in Israel, and he was on a kibbutz. And I came to visit him. And I saw there a lot of young German-- young, German people who did not go through the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked them, what are you doing here? And they told me, we love this country. We love to be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can never forgive a person my age, I-- because I don't trust him. Because I do no know what part he played during the war. My age. But a young one who did not experience the war, I can learn to forgive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is my message. To live in peace. To live together. To live as brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like also to read, but I cannot read, so I am going to talk to you about-- about a partisan hymn that was written by a partisan. His name was Kaczerginski. And he survived the war, but he perished on a plane accident in Argentina. And he wrote, 'Never say that it is the last road. Never say that you cannot see the light at the end of the tunnel. Because better days might come. Peace might come.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Mira, thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much for sharing your story with us. Now I think we're going to be joined by one of your sons for a few minutes.
